answer:
**In the opening chapter (Chapter 1) of *Traité de Législation, Vol. I*, Charles Comte introduces the analytical method—defined as observing facts, classifying them in natural order, and tracing how some arise from others—as the proper approach to studying legislation and morality, rather than inventing systems or reasoning from abstract principles.**[^1]

He explains that his goal is not to propose laws or critique specific institutions, but to examine causes of a people's prosperity, decline, or stagnation through this empirical process.[^2] Comte emphasizes that the method is proven in natural sciences and parts of moral sciences like political economy and the study of human understanding (citing Locke, Condillac, Adam Smith, and J.-B. Say), yielding reliable progress and truth.[^3]

Yet, he observes, it is rejected in legislation and morality by theologians (who see divine rules as sufficient, fearing analysis undermines faith), philosophers (who favor innate sentiment, imagination-based systems, or justice/duty over facts), and jurisconsults (who rely on unchallenged "natural law" maxims).[^4] Comte defends its universal applicability: moral phenomena (actions, habits, institutions) can be observed like physical ones, producing sciences via precise description rather than hypothesis or eloquence.[^5] True scholars follow facts impartially, letting truth's force prevail without force or exhortation, unlike system-builders.[^6]

He contrasts this with difficulties in moral sciences (e.g., inability to experiment on peoples, reliance on history), but insists facts suffice for progress, aided by recent advances in related fields and revolutions providing new data.[^7]

Comte's Chapter 2 then elaborates directly on the analytical method's effects, but Chapter 1 establishes its foundational role and defends its adoption for legislation.[^8]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation, Vol. I*, Ch. 1 [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-6a39c9).

[^2]: Ibid., [#p-6a39c9](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-6a39c9).

[^3]: Ibid., [#p-87c578](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-87c578) to [#p-e9c962](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-e9c962).

[^4]: Ibid., [#p-83dd97](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-83dd97) to [#p-a6513b](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-a6513b).

[^5]: Ibid., [#p-cf2bef](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-cf2bef) to [#p-ec68ff](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-ec68ff).

[^6]: Ibid., [#p-236350](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-236350) to [#p-54c81d](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/#p-54c81d).

[^7]: Ibid., [whole chapter](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/01-chapitre-premier/translation/).

[^8]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation, Vol. I*, Ch. 2 [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/chapters/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/02-de-la-methode-analytique-appliquee-a-l-etude-de-la/translation/).
